channel.title: Avery Regier's Sports Blog
channel.description: A Website About Sports and Data

item.title: Who’s leading the WNBA in 2026?
item.dc:creator: Avery Regier
item.description:
With March Madness, one of the most-watched sporting events, just rounding out the basketball season, fans are getting ready to watch the NBA draft and prepping for the new season. While lots of eyes are on men’s basketball, let’s not forget about the WNBA. Let’s be honest, we’ve all seen the Caitlin Clark and Angel Reese feud on our social media, but what about the rest of the teams? Where do they stand going into the next season?

This past season ended with the No. 2 seed, the Las Vegas Aces, winning the playoffs, and the No. 4 seed, the Phoenix Mercury, landing in the runner-up spot. The playoffs consisted of three separate rounds: a best-of-three first round, a best-of-five second round and a best-of-seven finals.

Wehoop, a R package designed to scrape WNBA data stats, from team box-scores, player box-scores and even play-by-plays, collected data from this past year to show the amount of rebounds and turnovers each team had throughout the season.

Code 
library(tidyverse)
library(wehoop)
library(gt)
wnba_pbp <- wnba_team_box <- wehoop::load_wnba_team_box()
table <- load_wbb_team_box(seasons = 2026) |> 
  group_by(team_display_name) |> 
  summarize(
    TotalTO = sum(total_turnovers),
    TotalRB = sum(total_rebounds)
  ) |> 
  select(team_display_name, TotalTO, TotalRB) |> 
  mutate_at(vars(-team_display_name), as.numeric) |>
  top_n(10,wt = TotalRB) |> 
  rename(Team = team_display_name) |> 
  arrange(desc(TotalTO))

table |> 
  gt() |> 
  cols_label(
    TotalRB = "Total Rebounds",
    TotalTO = "Total Turnovers"
  )|>
  tab_header(
    title = "Which team had better control of the ball?",
    subtitle = "BYU had the most turnovers in the 2025 season. The Trojans had the most rebounds."
  )|> tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", weight = "bold", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("title")
  ) |> tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("subtitle")
  ) |>
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = md("**By:** Avery Regier  |  **Source:** wehoop: The SportsDataverse's R Package for WNBA's Basketball Data")
  )|>
  tab_style(
     locations = cells_column_labels(columns = everything()),
     style = list(
       cell_borders(sides = "bottom", weight = px(3)),
       cell_text(weight = "bold", size=12)
     )
   )|>
  opt_row_striping() |> 
  opt_table_lines("none") |> 
tab_style(
  style= list(
    cell_fill(color = "#002E5D"),
    cell_text(color = "#FFFFFF")
  ),
  locations = cells_body(
    rows = Team == "BYU Cougars"
  )
) |> 
  tab_style(
  style= list(
    cell_fill(color = "#8a2432"),
    cell_text(color = "#FFFFFF")
  ),
  locations = cells_body(
    rows = Team == "Troy Trojans"
  )
)
Which team had better control of the ball?
BYU had the most turnovers in the 2025 season. The Trojans had the most rebounds.
Team Total Turnovers Total Rebounds
BYU Cougars 641 1545
Troy Trojans 559 1647
Oklahoma Sooners 557 1642
Idaho Vandals 536 1640
TCU Horned Frogs 519 1563
South Carolina Gamecocks 514 1698
LSU Tigers 511 1683
Louisville Cardinals 495 1514
UCLA Bruins 489 1611
Texas Longhorns 472 1523
By: Avery Regier | Source: wehoop: The SportsDataverse’s R Package for WNBA’s Basketball Data

Adding up all the rebounds and turnovers throughout the 2025 season, BYU had the most amount of turnovers, which means they lost the ball the most to the defensive team before being able to take a shot. The Trojans had the most amount of rebounds, meaning their players secured possession of the ball the most after a missed field goal or three-point shot.

The playoffs are the postseason event for the WNBA. While only two teams made it all the way to the end, several teams were in the early stages of the playoffs. But why did the final two teams make it to the end? Point differential shows the numerical gap between total points scored and the total points allowed by a team.

Code 
library(ggbump)
rankings <- wnba_team_box <- wehoop::load_wnba_team_box()
seasontype <- rankings |> 
  filter(season_type == "3") |> 
  mutate(differential = team_score- opponent_team_score) |> 
  group_by(team_display_name) |> 
  mutate(cum_diff = cumsum(differential)) |> 
  rename(Team = team_display_name)
ggplot()+
  geom_step(data=seasontype, aes(x=game_date, y=cum_diff, group=Team, color=Team))+
  labs(
    x="Post-Season Game Dates", 
    y="Cumulative Point Differential", 
    title="Who has more point differential in the post season?", 
    subtitle="The Las Vegas Aces showed the biggest differential in comparison to the other teams that made \nit to the playoffs. The Aces and the Phoenix Mercury made it the farthest in the playoffs. ", 
    caption="Source: wehoop | By Avery Regier"
    ) + 
  theme_minimal()+ 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )

The Aces, by far, had the best differential throughout the playoffs, showing they’re able to get the points and keep the opposing team from scoring.

Obviously, the success of the team is dependent on its athletes. Several awards to these players are handed out each year, including the status of player of the year and rookie of the year. These are some of the most prestigious awards to be handed out.

Code 
awards <- read_csv("wnbaawards.csv")
Rows: 21 Columns: 4
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr (3): award, team, player
dbl (1): year

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
Code 
awards |> 
  group_by(year)
# A tibble: 21 × 4
# Groups:   year [11]
    year award                 team             player              
   <dbl> <chr>                 <chr>            <chr>               
 1  2025 AP Player of the Year Las Vegas Aces   "A'ja Wilson"       
 2  2025 Rookie of the Year    Dallas Wings     "Paige Bueckers"    
 3  2024 AP Player of the Year Las Vegas Aces   "A'ja Wilson"       
 4  2024 Rookie of the Year    Indiana Fever    "Caitlin Clark"     
 5  2023 AP Player of the Year New York Liberty "Breanna Stewart"   
 6  2023 Rookie of the Year    Indiana Fever    "Aliyah Boston"     
 7  2022 AP Player of the Year Seattle Storm    "Breanna Stewart"   
 8  2022 Rookie of the Year    Atlanta Dream    "Rhyne Howard"      
 9  2021 AP Player of the Year Connecticut      "Sun\tJonquel Jones"
10  2021 Rookie of the Year    New York Liberty "Michaela Onyenwere"
# ℹ 11 more rows
Code 
cc <- awards |> 
  filter(
    team == "Indiana Fever"
  )
ggplot()+
  geom_bar(data=awards, aes(x=team))+
  geom_bar(data=cc, aes(x=team),fill="#C8102E")+
  coord_flip()+ 
  labs(
    x="", 
    y="Number of Awards", 
    title="Awards per team from the past 10 years", 
    subtitle="These teams each had a player of the year or rookie of the year award within the past decade. Highlighted is the \nIndiana Fever, who current women's basketball phenomenom, Caitlin Clark, plays for. Clark earned ROTY in 2024.", 
    caption="Source: Across the Timeline: WNBA Awards | By Avery Regier"
    )+
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )

The Las Vegas Aces and the Seattle Storm have the most athletes with award wins in the past 10 years. This is making the Las Vegas Aces stand out even more, showing that they have the best differential in the postseason and they have the players to back that up. Caitlin is one of two athletes for the Indiana Fever to claim an award within the past decade. Angel claims she is also the reason we watch women’s sports, but where’s she at?

With the regular season about to start, it looks like the Las Vegas Aces have a good shot to come out strong and steal the show. They have proven themselves this past season and fans are excited to see what is to come for next year. The 30th WNBA season starts May 8 with a matchup against the Toronto Tempo and Portland Fire, with a finals rematch coming on May 9.

item.title: Post With Code
item.dc:creator: Harlow Malloc
item.description:
This is a post with executable code.

1 + 1
[1] 2

item.title: Welcome To My Blog
item.dc:creator: Tristan O'Malley
item.description:
This is the first post in a Quarto blog. Welcome!

Since this post doesn’t specify an explicit image, the first image in the post will be used in the listing page of posts.
